2002-08-01.title: Road deaths ignite Korean anti-Americanism
2002-08-01.abstract: UIJONGBU, South Korea:—  On a concrete wall surrounding Camp Red Cloud, a small U.S. Army post here, an identification photograph of Sergeant Mark Walker stares blankly from dozens of posters proclaiming him "wanted" for the "crime of murder."

2002-08-01.title: World Briefing | Europe: Russia: Orthodox Church Berates Vatican
2002-08-01.abstract: Russian Orthodox Church says Roman Catholic Church is proselytizing in Russia, and that this activity dooms any dialogue; Vatican denies seeking to convert Orthodox believers; two churches have had angry exchanges since Feb, when Roman church formed four dioceses in Russia(S).

2002-08-01.title: Rare Place For Jews And Arabs To Meet
2002-08-01.abstract: Hebrew University in Jerusalem is one of few places where Israel's Jews and its Arabs mix freely and debate their differences; students say relations can be strained, but they have remained peaceful on campus (S)

2002-08-01.title: World Briefing | Europe: Germany: Berlin Official Quits
2002-08-01.abstract: Gregor Gysi, municipal economy minister in Berlin and prominent member of party of reform Communists, resigns two days after admitting making personal use of bonus air miles from official flights during his time as member of Parliament (S)

2002-08-01.title: Bolstering Faith of Indians, Pope Gives Mexico a Saint
2002-08-01.abstract: Pope John Paul II canonizes Juan Diego, Indian convert to Catholicism in 16th century, during lavishly staged Mass in Mexico City that mingles flourishes from ancient Aztecs with traditional hymns; pope makes emphatic appeal to indigenous people who have abandoned Roman Catholic Church; Indians dance through Basilica of Guadalupe in honor of first indigenous saint in Americas; canonnization seen as signal moment in church's fight for future in Latin America, where evangelical Protestants are increasingly making inroads, especially among indigenous people; there is considerable debate in Mexico about whether Juan Diego was real man or convenient marketing tool for Catholic faith; photo (M)

2002-08-02.title: Study in Israel: American Jews Feel the Tremors and Worry
2002-08-02.abstract: Deaths of five Americans in bombing of cafeteria at Hebrew University have sent tremors through world of American Jews who have been sending their children almost ritually to study in Israel's colleges and yeshivas as way of cementing ties to land; so many families send high school graduates or college juniors to Israel for study, both secular and religious, that it has become proud rite of passage in some circles; John Fisher, admissions official at Yeshiva University in Manhattan, says he expects latest attack to set off soul-searching, particularly among parents, who seem more worried than students who actually go; photo (M)

2002-08-02.title: A Blow to Security, and to Tolerance
2002-08-02.abstract: Fortresslike campus of Hebrew University on Mount Scopus in Jerusalem gave students and professors sense of security, but that is gone following bombing of campus cafeteria in which seven people were killed and dozens wounded; attack has challenged efforts to maintain diverse academic community in environment rife with bloodshed and hate; bombing victims include Jews, Arabs and foreigners, including five Americans; foreign students are grappling with loss of compatriots killed in attack, waiting for news of wounded and debating with anxious relatives back home whether they should stay in Israel; photo; map (M)

2002-08-02.title: President of China to Visit Bush's Ranch in Texas
2002-08-02.abstract: WASHINGTON, Aug. 2 — President Bush will welcome President Jiang Zemin of China to his ranch at Crawford, Tex., on Oct. 25, the White House announced today.

2002-08-02.title: Scandal Over Frequent-Flier Miles Shakes Up German Politics
2002-08-02.abstract: Four more German politicians are accused of misusing frequent-flier miles; widening scandal has already brought high-level resignations of Gregor Gysi, deputy mayor for Berlin, and Cem Ozdemir, Green Party spokesman, and could affect Chancellor Gerhard Schroder's bid to retain power in Sept elections; at issue is their use of frequent-flier miles earned on the job to buy tickets for people close to them (M)

2002-08-02.title: Booby-Trapped Lunch Box Kills a Worker in Northern Ireland
2002-08-02.abstract: Construction worker at disused British Army base in Londonderry, Northern Ireland, dies after picking up bomb disguised as lunch box; police say Real IRA, dissident group, is reponsible; victim, David Caldwell, is third person to be killed this year in continuing sectarian strife in Northern Ireland; labor union leaders express frustration at inability of politicians to stem continuing violence, especially against civilians in workplace; labor movement plans rally in Belfast (M)

2002-08-03.title: Russia Says It May Reconsider Its Nuclear Plant Deal With Iran
2002-08-03.abstract: Minister of Russia's nuclear energy agency, Aleksandr Y Rumyantsev, after three days of meetings in Moscow with Bush administration officials led by Sec Spencer Abraham, says Russia will weigh 'political' factors in deciding whether to continue building nuclear reactors in Iran, which United States fears could be used covertly to build nuclear arms; insists Russia's cooperation with Iran is limited to civilian development of nuclear energy (M)

2002-08-03.title: World Briefing | Asia: Afghanistan: Protests And Fighting
2002-08-03.abstract: Thousands of people protest new Afghan government for fourth day in Khost, where United States military continues operations to hunt Al Qaeda and Taliban members; back local warlord Bacha Khan Zadran, who demands to be consulted on appointments of governors in region; as many as 70 people are reportedly killed in fighting among ethnic Tajik and Pashtun soldiers outside Herat; Pashtuns appeal for help from central government (S)

2002-08-03.title: North and South Korea Ease Into Discussions on Reconciliation
2002-08-03.abstract: North Korea intensifies its diplomatic offensive, surprising American command with request for first meeting in 20 months between North Korean and American general, to talk about June 29 Yellow Sea naval skirmish with South Korea; demands revision of line south of which North Korea boats are banned by South Korea and United States; team of South Korean officials goes to North Korean resort for talks with North Koreans on agenda for cabinet-level talks (M)

2002-08-03.title: World Briefing | Europe: Russia: Headscarves Banned From ID Photos
2002-08-03.abstract: Tatarstan court rejects suit by three Muslim woman seeking right to wear headscarves in their photos for internal passports all Russians must carry (S)

2002-08-03.title: No Headline
2002-08-03.abstract: Bishop James M Ryan, Roman Catholic missionary who was bishop of Santarem, Brazil, dies at age 89; photo (M)

2002-08-04.title: Defying China, Taiwan Leader Backs a Vote On Sovereignty
2002-08-04.abstract: Pres Chen Shui-bian of Taiwan says separate countries now exist on either side of Taiwan Strait and voices support for legislation authorizing referendum to declare Taiwan independent of China; statements are certain to anger Beijing; his comments are made in video teleconference with pro-independence Taiwanese in Tokyo; he also lends his support to pro-independence forces in his Democratic Progressive Party who want national Legislature to approve law making plebiscite on independence possible; this is closest Chen has come to endorsing independence since taking office two years ago; he does not actually say when or even whether referendum should be held (M)

2002-08-04.title: Two Koreas Agree to First Cabinet-Level Talks in Almost a Year
2002-08-04.abstract: North and South Korea agree to hold first cabinet-level talks between them in nearly year on wide range of critical issues including skirmish in Yellow Sea in June that left five South Koreans dead (M)

2002-08-04.title: Turks May Face Long Road to the European Union
2002-08-04.abstract: European Union's commitment to Turkey remains in doubt, even if Turkey abolishes death penalty and expands civil rights of Kurds; beneath veneer of Turkish democracy lies society where generals, who have seized power three times, still hold sway; freedom of expression is restricted to muzzle Kurdish separatists; torture remains accepted weapon of securityforces in certain circumstances (M)

2002-08-04.title: Uganda's Terror Crackdown Multiplies the Suffering
2002-08-04.abstract: Government of Uganda has launched its own version of American-led effort to fight terrorism, after Bush administration included Ugandan rebel group Lord's Resistance Army on its list of terrorist groups; government's hard-line approach was at first supported by population weary of war; many are having second thoughts as rebels step up their attacks in response to government raids; in Gulu, huts of thousands of people have been destroyed in recent fighting between government soldiers and rebels; map (M)

2002-08-04.title: U.S.-Mideast Connection: The News Becomes Less Foreign
2002-08-04.abstract: Analysis of America's personal, even intimate connection to Israeli-Palestinian conflict; five Americans are among seven people killed at Hebrew Univ by bomb in cafeteria; US Amb Daniel C Kurtzer is himself graduate of university, which depends heavily on American donors; of estimated 80,000 people living in Israel with American citizenship, half are Palestinian and half are Israeli; people on both sides of conflict are seeking visas to go to US during conflict; photo (M)

2002-08-05.title: 3 Die in Gunfight at East Jerusalem Market
2002-08-05.abstract: 19-year-old Palestinian gunman and Arab bystander die in gun battle with paramilitary Israeli border police in Arab East Jerusalem; battle erupts after gunman opens fire on truck beloning to Israeli telephone company, killing security guard and injuring driver; photo (M)

2002-08-05.title: China Fumes At Taipei Chief Over Vote Plan
2002-08-05.abstract: Chinese government angrily condemns as 'brazen provocation' recent speech by Taiwan Pres Chen Shui-bian, who said China and Taiwan are two separate countries and endorsed legislation to allow referendum on Taiwanese independence (S)

2002-08-05.title: BURST OF ATTACKS FROM PALESTINIANS CAUSES 14 DEATHS
2002-08-05.abstract: Burst of widespread Palestinian violence in defiance of Israel's toughening military crackdown leaves 14 dead and scores wounded in West Bank; suicide bombing of bus in Galilee at start of workweek in which 9 people die is first of five attacks, that include shootout near Damascus Gate in Jerusalem's Old City that left three people dead, two ambushes that wounded seven Israelis and ambush near Nablus in which married couple die; bus attack is condemned by Pres Bush; Israeli government vows to pursue bombers 'without mercy;' blame Palestinian leadesr Yasir Arafat for escalating violence; Israeli Army adopts new tactic aimed at deterring suicide bombers; blows up nine houses of families of past bombers around Nablus, Jenin and Hebron, in addition to two other houses destroyed earlier; map; photos (M)

2002-08-05.title: 6 Killed at Christian School in Pakistan
2002-08-05.abstract: Four gunmen dressed in Western clothes shot their way into an isolated Christian missionary school and gunned down six Pakistanis.

2002-08-05.title: EASING ITS STANCE, U.S. OFFERS LOANS TO NATION IN CRISIS
2002-08-05.abstract: Bush administration softens stance against financial aid for struggling countries and will grant up to $1.5 billion in short-term loans to Uruguay; loan will be first of kind by administration to assist other country directly in financial crisis; administration's earlier opposition to such assistance, which it maintained is often wasted and is unfair to American taxpayers, discussed; administration felt it unwise to lend money to countries facing unsustainable debt loads until they undertake steps to restructure finances; decision is made following passage of emergency legislation by Uruguayan Congress to help quell nation's economic crisis; administration officials say they expect loan to be repaid within days, once Uruguay receives longer-term package of loans from World Bank, IMF and Inter-American Development Bank that will bring total assiatance to $3.8 billion; decision comes as Treas Sec Paul H O'Neill arrives in Brazil on first leg of trip aimed at helping reassure Latin American officials about US attention to troubled regional economy; says loan will help Uruguay address intense external pressures, and is made in recognition of concrete actions by Uruguay to address them (M)

2002-08-06.title: Warlord Pushes for Control Of a Corner of Afghanistan
2002-08-06.abstract: Interview with Padsha Khan Zadran, warlord in Khost region of Afghanistan who is at odds with central government of Pres Hamid Karzai in Kabul, which is threatening to use force if necessary to end lawlessness and violence in region; map; photos (M)

2002-08-06.title: Israeli Supreme Court Approves Force Against Palestinian Militants
2002-08-06.abstract: Israel's Supreme Court upheld the practice of demolishing homes of Palestinian militants without warning today as troops killed a West Bank militiaman suspected of sending two suicide bombers to Tel Aviv.

2002-08-06.title: World Briefing | Europe: Vatican: Female ''Priests'' Excommunicated
2002-08-06.abstract: Vatican announces excommunication of seven women who have been calling themselves priests since ordination ceremony in June that Vatican branded illegitimate; ordered women to renounce claims to being ordained and confess error but they refused; women are from Austria, Germany and United States (S)

2002-08-06.title: U.N. and Congress Rebuff Iraq's Inspection Invitation
2002-08-06.abstract: UN Security Council and Sec Gen Kofi Annan agree that weapons inspectors will not return to Iraq to restart talks after break of almost four years unless government of Pres Saddam Hussein agrees to abide by organization's rules on how inspections must be conducted; leading members of Congress reject Iraq's invitation to visit country and to inspect suspected weapons sites for themselves; Democrats and Republicans are equally dismissive; photo (M)

2002-08-06.title: Congo Peace Accord Evokes Cautious Hopes
2002-08-06.abstract: Latest agreement to bring peace to Congo, where at least 2.5 million people have perished in civil strife over last four years, raises cautious hope that complicated conflict may be nearing end; few in capital of Kinshasa expect immediate end to hostilities since either side is unlikely to stick to letter of pact, which calls for Congo to crack down on Hutu militias blamed for mass killings of ethnic Tutsi and Rwanda to withdraw troops from Congo; map; photo (M)

2002-08-07.title: World Briefing | Europe: France: Far-Right Group Banned
2002-08-07.abstract: France outlaws Radical Unity, rightist group of French neo-Nazi who tried to assassinate Pres Jacques Chirac in July (S)

2002-08-07.title: Bush Signs Trade Bill, Restoring Broad Presidential Authority
2002-08-07.abstract: Pres Bush signs 'fast-track trade' bill restoring presidential authority to negotiate trade deals with foreign countries without having them changed by Congress; says he will move quickly on trade deals with Chile, Singapore and Morocco and negotiate Free Trade Area of the Americas; Bush then leaves for monthlong vacation at Texas ranch (M)

2002-08-07.title: After Pakistan Raid, 3 Mysterious Suicides
2002-08-07.abstract: Three men blow themselves up with grenades when Pakistani police stop them 25 miles from Christian missionary school where gunmen killed six people; link is unclear; authorities focus probe on new coalition of Islamic militants, Lashkar-e-Omar; more than 145 Australian, European and American children survived attack on school in Jhika Gali (M)

2002-08-07.title: Israeli Court Upholds Blowing Up Houses
2002-08-07.abstract: Israeli Supreme Court says army may blow up family homes of suicide bombers without notice, because country is in midst of war and giving notice could endanger soldiers; Israeli forces kill two Palestinian militants near Jenin, including one blamed for bombs in foreign workers' district of Tel Aviv; police avert at least one attack in Jerusalem when two sets of explosive devices packed with metal are found near tunnel; 16-year-old Palestinian girl is charged in court with planning attack; photo; nerves are still shattered by attacks that took 13 lives in less than 24 hours and by bomb at Hebrew University that killed 7; reaction is furious to news reports on slow pace of work on 225-mile separation fence along West Bank; Min Benjamin Ben-Eliezer reports 140 would-be suicide bombers in custody; little progress reported in his talks with Palestinian security officials (M)

2002-08-07.title: South Africa May Bar AIDS Drug in Childbirth
2002-08-07.abstract: South Africa's drug safety board considers reversing decision to allow AIDS drug nevirapine to be used to prevent transmission of HIV from mothers to newborn babies; report stirs outrage among AIDS activists; one dose of drug taken during labor can reduce transmission risk as much as fifty percent (S)

2002-08-08.title: Colombian Sticks to Plans Despite Shelling That Killed 19
2002-08-08.abstract: President Álvaro Uribe Vélez travelled to the northern province today, even though his inauguration was marked by an attack.

2002-08-08.title: CHALLENGES OF DEVELOPMENT: Summit aims, again, for a better world
World Briefing | Asia: Pakistan: Police Investigate Suicide Raid
2002-08-08.abstract: Investigators examine body of one of three men who blew themselves up with grenades 25 miles from missionary school in Pakistan attacked earlier in week; bodies of other two men washed down river; three reportedly said they carried out attack (S)

2002-08-08.title: Hitler, It Seems, Loved Money and Died Rich
2002-08-08.abstract: New German television documentary focuses on Adolf Hitler's personal finances, portraying him as man who was abstemious in his public image yet liked to live grandly; Ingo Helm, who made program Hitler's Money, says Hitler spent millions in lavish gifts and payments to buy loyalty of politicians and businessmen and to keep them dependent on him; says Hitler made few distinctions between his own money and that of Nazi Party and even state; says that from time he became chancellor until his death in 1945, Hitler received equivalent of $3 billion in corporate payments; after war, his property and assets were given to state of Bavaria by Allied Control Commission; photo (M)

2002-08-08.title: Firing of former officer unfair, court rules : Whistleblower upheld in UN Bosnia police case
2002-08-08.abstract: PARIS:—  A British tribunal has ruled that a former member of the UN police force in Bosnia was unfairly fired after she reported to her superiors that colleagues in the police force used women and children as sex slaves in connivance with Balkan traffickers.

2002-08-09.title: Sharon Calls Palestinians 'Corrupt Terrorists'
2002-08-09.abstract: Prime Minister Ariel Sharon of Israel called Yasir Arafat's Palestinian Authority "a gang of corrupt terrorists and assassins" in a televised speech on Thursday.

2002-08-09.title: Taliban Threatens F.B.I. 'Spy' List
2002-08-09.abstract: Letters signed by 'Taliban Islamic government' threatening to kill anyone who cooperates with United States are left at 20 homes in town of Wana, where 10 Pakistani soldiers recently died in firefight as they tried to arrest 10 suspected Qaeda members; letters, handwritten in Pashto, warn that 'each spy' would be hunted own and killed like man slain last week after being accused of spying for FBI (S)

2002-08-09.title: World Briefing: Middle East
2002-08-09.abstract: IRAN: 2 More Reformist Newspapers Banned ; EGYPT: Police Officers Jailed In Torture Death ;.

2002-08-09.title: Milosevic Trial Leaves Most Serbs Cynical
2002-08-09.abstract: Most Serbs are convinced that war crimes trial of former leader Slobodan Milosevic is politically motivated; hardly anybody in Serbia laments passing of his authoritarian government, but defiance runs strong in Serbia's history; many there relish sight of Milosevic stonewalling prosecutors at United Nations tribunal in The Hague and conducting his own defense with bluster that threatens to make farce of proceedings; Milosevic's medical condition may prevent trial from continung; even if it does, Serbs will take some convincing of their former leader's guilt; photo (M)

2002-08-09.title: World Briefing: Middle East
2002-08-09.abstract: IRAN: 2 More Reformist Newspapers Banned ; EGYPT: Police Officers Jailed In Torture Death ;.

2002-08-10.title: More Look for La Dolce Vita in the Trevi Fountain
2002-08-10.abstract: Rome police arrest woman as she wades into Trevi Fountain and scoops up nearly $200 in coins tourists had thrown there; say she was inspired by news reports about Roberto Cercelletta, mentally unstable man who has regularly plundered pool beneath fountain for 34 years; fear more copycats; she is charged with trying to damage public monument, not theft, because coins, which are supposed to go to charity, are not anyone's property (M)

2002-08-10.title: U.S. Ties Military Aid to Peacekeepers' Immunity
2002-08-10.abstract: Bush administration, making use of provision of new antiterrorism law, warns foreign diplomats that their nations could lose all American military aid if they join International Criminal Court without pledging to protect Americans serving in their nations from its reach; law authorizes president to waive provision when it is in national interest; exempts NATO members and other major allies; is intended to force many nations to sign bilateral accords not to extradite Americans to new court; Romania and Israel have signed such accord (M)

2002-08-10.title: William R. Crawford -- U.S. Envoy and Mideast Specialist, 74
2002-08-10.abstract: William R Crawford, retired American career diplomat and expert on Mideast and Cyprus, dies at age 74 (M)

2002-08-10.title: Shifting Colombia's Aid: U.S. Focuses on Rebels
2002-08-10.abstract: Little-noticed provision of antiterrorism package newly signed by Pres Bush authorizes new Colombian Pres Alvaro Uribe Velez to use nearly $1.7 billion in American military aid directly against leftist rebels and right-wing paramilitaries; previous guidelines limited use of American-supplied helicopters and soldiers trained by United States trooops to anti-drug operations; policy shift comes at time of escalating guerrilla violence (M)

2002-08-10.title: South African Village, Fearing AIDS, Trusts God More Than Drugs
2002-08-10.abstract: AIDS sufferers in remote Hlabisa, South Africa, where about 35 percent of adults are believed to be infected with HIV, turn to faith healer Rev Solomon Mahlangu and church he founded; his followers believe faith alone will protect them, refusing precautions urged by AIDS counselors; map; photos (M)

2002-08-11.title: Burning Issues, Scalding Times for Italy's Premier
2002-08-11.abstract: Prime Min Silvio Berlusconi's effective control over most of Italy's national television stations has come under renewed fire; his decision to extend his stint as country's foreign minister, second hat he wears, has exacerbated worries that he is brashly consolidating power; latest econonomic figures are also causing him problems; political analysts say his record over last 14 months is mixed and inconclusive one, with triumphs as well as some setbacks; photos (M)

2002-08-11.title: Iraqi Opposition Gets U.S. Pledge To Oust Hussein for a Democracy
2002-08-11.abstract: Vice Pres Dick Cheney, vacationing in Wyoming, confers with Iraqi opposition leaders visiting Washington via video conferencing and reportedly says Bush administration is determined to oust Pres Saddam Hussein and replace him with democratic government; leaders also meet with Defense Sec Donald Rumsfeld and Joint Chiefs of Staff chairman Gen Richard B Myers; say talks deal with military situation and how Iraq will be governed if Hussein is deposed; photo (M)

2002-08-11.title: Turkmen Leader, Wishing to Be August, Settles for January
2002-08-11.abstract: Personality cult envelops Turkmenistan's first and only leader, Pres Saparmurat Niyazov; cities and streets, mosques, factories and airports all bear his name, and his portrait appears on country's money, on banners and posters, on bottles of vodka and packets of tea; now Niyazov wants to rename months of year using his name and title of book he has written; photo (M)

2002-08-11.title: Pakistani Who Died in Deadly Attack on Christian Hospital Is Identified
2002-08-11.abstract: Pakistani police say they have identified one of three attackers who killed three nurses and injured 23 people during raid on Christian hospital in Taxila, near Islamabad; say he is Kamran Mir from Rawalpindi; say they believe he was member of Islamic militant group, but they do not know which one (M)

2002-08-11.title: Brazilians Find Political Cost for Help From I.M.F.
2002-08-11.abstract: Brazil is in midst of important national election; it has just engaged $30 billion lifeline from International Monetary Fund that imposes strict policies on next government; outgoing Pres Fernando Henrique Cardoso says loan will give Brazil vital oxygen, but to some Brazilians, IMF is strangling economy with its restraints; loan comes with unusual strings--$24 billion of it will be deliverd next year only if new government meets certain budgetary targets; photo (M)

2002-08-12.title: Shanghai Journal; A Chinese Crematory's Work Is a Matter of Life
2002-08-12.abstract: World's largest crematory is in Shanghai, China, city where 100,000 residents die each year; as many as 400 bodies a day are cremated in 24 incinerators; China began encouraging cremation decades ago when it became clear country could not continue burying people in shrinking available land of its expanding cities; cremation is enforced in cities, but it poses ecological challenges; photo (M)

2002-08-12.title: Arafat Calls U.S. Meetings 'Very Positive'; Sharon Sees 'Trick'
2002-08-12.abstract: Yasir Arafat says meetings between his representatives and American officials in Washington last week were 'very positive,' then quickly says that Israel is not looking for peace; Prime Min Ariel Sharon charges that Palestinians' provisional agreement to Israeli security plan is 'a trick'; security plan, put forward by Defense Min Benyamin Ben-Eliezer, comes under fire from several Israeli ministers on right; plan calls for tentative pullback of Israeli troops in Gaza as test of whether Palestinians can stop attacks on Israel; talks have broken down, with Palestinians accusing Israelis of reneging on agreement to include Bethlehem in initial pullback and Israelis saying Palestinians are insisting on Ramallah; seven of eight Palestinian cities in West Bank are now under control of Israeli troops; spate of violence continues on ground; map (M)

2002-08-12.title: The future of cities : Learning to manage urban sprawl
2002-08-12.abstract: PARIS:—  This is the second of five articles leading up to the World Summit for Sustainable Development, which begins Aug. 26 in Johannesburg.

2002-08-12.title: Easing of Marijuana Laws Angers Many Britons
2002-08-12.abstract: Six-month experiment in loosening England's national drug laws in Brixton section of South London pleases many marijuana smokers but leaves many other residents feeling that their neighborhood has turned into open-air drug bazaar; partly as result of Brixton's trial, government has announced plans to downgrade criminal penalties for smoking pot in country where estimated five million people are habitual users; plan is acknowledgment that drugs like heroin and cocaine are far more harmful than marijuana, but it gets mixed reviews and raises host of questions about loosening marijuana laws; photos; map (M)

2002-08-12.title: Bush Team Is Divided Over Getting Tougher With Saudis
2002-08-12.abstract: Some members of Bush administration are pushing for tough new American posture toward Saudi Arabia in wake of Sept 11 attacks, in which 15 of 19 hijackers were Saudis; Saudi foreign minister, Prince Saud al-Faisal, emphasizes Saudi cooperation with Bush administration's campaign against terrorism but says his kingdom does not want American forces to use Saudi soil for any military campaign against Iraq; American military strategists have devised contingency plans for staging combat operations in Iraq without Saudi support, but it would be easier to have cooperation; Sen Carl Levin claims Saudi Arabia has not been consistent ally of US and Sen Fred Thompson characterizes Saudi reluctance to support action against Iraq as self-preservation to blunt criticism from religious extremists within Saudi Arabia (M)

2002-08-13.title: World Briefing | Africa: Kenya: Aides Fired For Opposing President
2002-08-13.abstract: Kenyan Pres Daniel arap Moi fires Environment Min Joseph Kamotho and assistant minister Fred Gumo, who have refused to support his chosen successor Uhuru Kenyatta; Energy Min Raila Odinga begins campaign for nomination of governing Kenya African National Union party (S)

2002-08-13.title: Islamic Militants Rebuff Move to Curb Bombings
2002-08-13.abstract: Islamic militants today rejected an effort to end some attacks on civilians, as Israel announced plans to try a Palestinian leader for murder.

2002-08-13.title: World Briefing | Asia: Koreas: Talks Get Under Way
2002-08-13.abstract: North and South Korea begin first cabinet-level talks since Nov 2001, discussing construction of across-the-border rail line and reunions for families separated by Korean War; leader of North Korean delegation Kim Ryong Song is optimistic about outcome (S)

2002-08-13.title: Indian President, on His First Official Trip, Visits Site of Riots
2002-08-13.abstract: New Indian President Dr A P J Abdul Kalam visits riot-torn Gujarat state, politically sensitive terrain, on his first official trip; visit is first in memory by Indian president to site of Hindu-Muslim violence; said he wanted Gujarat to be first presidential destination since it is home to ashram of Gandhi, but area is also key political battleground; it is last large state controlled by Hindu nationalist Bharatriya Janata Party, which heads national government in New Delhi but has lost series of state elections; map (M)

2002-08-13.title: President of Iran Says U.S. Creates 'War-Like' Tension
2002-08-13.abstract: President Mohammed Khatami of Iran struck out at President Bush today during a visit to Afghanistan.

2002-08-14.title: In Diplomacy With North Korea, Some Progress, and a Setback
2002-08-14.abstract: North and South Korea make progress at ministerial talks in Seoul over uniting divided families and rebuilding road and rail links and North Korean general meets with American counterpart to discuss avoiding armed conflicts; even so, North Korea threatens to pull out of 1994 nuclear accord with US (M)

2002-08-14.title: Tens of Thousands Flee Prague As Floods Invade Historic Center
2002-08-14.abstract: Heavy rains lash Central Europe for seventh day; floodwaters sweep through historic center of Prague, Czech Republic, forcing tens of thousands to flee; swamp city's low-lying districts, threatening many architectural treasures; cities south of Prague are also submerged; Pres Vaclav Havel cuts short vacation in Portugal; death toll in Czech Republic rises to 9 and to 88 across Europe and Russia; meteorologists blame low pressure and relatively high temperatures for creating virtual water fountain above Central Europe; map; photos (M)

2002-08-14.title: World Briefing | Africa: Uganda: Rebels Release Kidnap Victims
2002-08-14.abstract: Lord's Resistance Army, Ugandan rebel group, releases 4 aid workers abducted last week in raid on refugee camp and nearly 100 women and children kidnapped from their villages earlier (S)

2002-08-14.title: World Briefing | Asia: Pakistan: U.S. Cultural Center Closes
2002-08-14.abstract: American officials report American cultural center in downtown Islamabad, Pakistan, has closed because of security concerns; nurse wounded in attack on Christian hospital near city dies, bringing number of women killed to five (S)

2002-08-14.title: OBITUARY : John Callcott, 64, foreign correspondent
2002-08-14.abstract: GENEVA:—  John Callcott, 64, a veteran United Press International foreign correspondent, died of cancer Monday in Geneva.

2002-08-15.title: Musharraf Assails Islamic Militants, but Stands Firm on Kashmir
2002-08-15.abstract: Pres Pervez Musharraf of Pakistan, in nationwide address, lashes out at Islamic militants for recent attacks on Christian school and hospital, calling acts 'shameful' and 'despicable' and vowing to 'break the back' of groups; Western diplomats conclude that Musharraf was aiming his harsh statement about militants at United States, since he spoke mostly in English; in matter of Kashmir, Musharraf vows that Pakistan will not back away from its demand for United Nations-monitored referendum to allow people of Kashmir to choose whether to join Pakistan or India or become independent; India adamantly opposes referendum and accuses Pakistan of supporting Islamic insurgents fighting against Indian rule in mostly Muslim region (M)

2002-08-15.title: Hong Kong Journal; Love and Money Bring Woe to a Rising Politician
2002-08-15.abstract: Antony Leung, Hong Kong's financial secretary and third-ranking official, is struggling for political survival because of his financial management, controversial economic views and recent marriage to China's most famous athlete--Fu Mingxia, four-time Olympic gold medal winner who is less than half his age; photo (M)

2002-08-15.title: The expanding reach of nongovernment aid
2002-08-15.abstract: This is the third of five articles leading up to the World Summit for Sustainable Development, which begins Aug. 26 in Johannesburg.

2002-08-15.title: Sept. 11 Families to Sue Saudi-Linked 'Interests'
2002-08-15.abstract: More than 300 relatives of victims of Sept 11 terror attacks prepare to file suit against various 'interests' associated with government of Saudi Arabia, alleging that they helped finance Osama bin Laden and his terrorist network; Allan Gerson, lawyer representing families, says suit will seek $300 billion in damages; refuses to identify Saudi 'interests' that will be named in suit, or to say if Saudi government itself would be among defendants; American government officials have long worried about extent of financial support provided by prominent Saudi officials to bin Laden and his Al Qaeda network (M)

2002-08-15.title: Georgia Hearing Heavy Footsteps From Russia's War in Chechnya
2002-08-15.abstract: Russia's war in Chechnya spills across its border with Georgia, worsening already tense relations between two countries; Georgian officials accuse Russian fighter jets and helicopters of repeatedly crossing into Georgian territory, evidently in pursuit of Chechen fighters; Russian aircraft reportedly bomb gorge in Georgia two miles from border; Russia denies crossing into Georgia, but senior Russian officials accuse Georgia of harboring rebels fighting Russian forces in Chechnya and demand that Georgia root out rebels using Georgian territory as base; some Russians suggest launching strikes even deeper into Georgia; Georgian officials discount possibility of full-scale Russian operation in Georgia, but fear futher incursions or raids by Russian aircraft; photos; map (M)

2002-08-16.title: London Journal; A Fence for the Orthodox Faces a Wall of Critics
2002-08-16.abstract: Orthodox Jews living in Golders Green suburb of London want to designate six-square mile area as Britain's first eruv, area where observant Jews are exempted from some Sabbath prohibitions against carrying keys or pushing strollers and wheelchairs; project has angered some non Jews and even secular Jews, and fray has generated charges of hypocrisy from one side and anti-Semitism on other; for 13 years, opponents have waged passionate campaign to prevent local authority from approving creation of eruv boundary using 84 posts up to 30 feet high and linked with 1,000 yards of fishing line to complete 11-mile perimeter; photos; map (M)

2002-08-16.title: Dresden, Battered by Elbe, Fights to Preserve Its Heritage
2002-08-16.abstract: Floodwaters of Elbe threaten Dresden's Baroque landmarks, including Zwinger Palace, home to many of city's art treasures; hundreds of museum workers, police officers and soldiers carry 4,000 paintings to upper floors of 19th-century palace after underground storage rooms flood; Elbe, already close to its 1845 record high, continues to rise as water that swamped Prague earlier this week heads downstream toward its eventual release into sea near Hamburg; main railroad station in Dresden is flooded by nearby Weisseritz River; 3,000 of city's 480,000 inhabitants are evacuated; Chancellor Gerhard Schroder promises help to region; photos; maps (M)

2002-08-16.title: TOP  REPUBLICANS BREAK  WITH  BUSH ON IRAQ STRATEGY
2002-08-16.abstract: Leading Republicans from Congress, State Dept and past administrations are breaking ranks with Pres Bush over his administration's high-profile planning for war with Iraq, saying administration has neither adequately prepared for military action nor made case that it is needed; say they favor eventual removal of Saddam Hussein, but that Bush is proceeding in way that risks alienating allies, creating greater instability in Middle East, and harming America's long-term interests; say administration has not shown that Iraq poses urgent threat to US; Brent Scowcroft, first Pres Bush's national security adviser, who helped build broad international coalition against Iraq in Persian Gulf War, writes opinion article in Wall Street Journal warning that attack on Iraq at this time would jeopardize, if not destroy, global counter-terrorist campaign; says attack might provoke Iraq to use chemicial or biological weapons in effort to trigger war between Israel and Arab world; Henry A Kissinger, in opinion article in Washington Post, makes long and complex argument about international complications of any military campaign; Sen Chuck Hagel, Republican who was among earliest voices to question Bush's approach to Iraq, claims CIA has 'absolutely no evidence' that Iraq possesses or will soon possess nuclear weapons (M)

2002-08-16.title: Zimbabwe Cracks Down on Whites Who Won't Quit Farms
2002-08-16.abstract: Over the past two days, more than a dozen white farmers have been arrested and charged with illegally occupying their land, leaders of the farmers' union say.

2002-08-16.title: A Celebration to Remember Liberation Unites Koreans for a Day
2002-08-16.abstract: Largest delegation of North Korean civilians in memory joins South Koreans in Seoul, South Korea, to celebrate anniversary of ending of World War II and Japanese colonial rule; 116 North Koreans wave 'unification' flags and chant 'Korea is one' at ceremony; celebration is kickoff to series of North-South civilian exchanges; it comes just six weeks after North and South Korea fought gun battle at sea; North Korea is eager to win restoration of food aid from South Korea and repair relations demaged by naval conflict (M)

2002-08-17.title: Zimbabwe Starts Arresting White Farmers Defying Eviction
2002-08-17.abstract: Government of Zimbabwe arrests more than dozen white farmers who have defied official eviction order that requires them to abandon their properties; farmers face up to two years in jail if convicted; police warn that more farmers will be arrested in coming days; photo (M)

2002-08-17.title: Attacks on Minority Faiths Rise in Post-Soviet Georgia
2002-08-17.abstract: Georgia is experiencing wave of violence against non-Orthodox faiths, particularly Protestant denominations that established themselves after fall of Soviet Union; Jehovah's Witnesses, Baptists, members of Assembly of God and members of Hare Krishna have been among those attacked; many ardent Orthodox followers regard growth of other denominations as threat to traditional dominance of Georgian Orthodox Church, which has increasingly become linked to nationalist causes; Pres Eduard Shevardnadze condemns violence and orders new measures to ensure rights of worshipers; photos of attack on Jehovah's Witness meeting hall in Kaspi; map (M)

2002-08-17.title: World Briefing | Asia: Kashmir: Indians Meet Separatists
2002-08-17.abstract: Delegation from Prime Min Atal Bihari Vajpayee offers separatist leaders in Jammu and Kashmir an invitation for talks, but is told that government must first release political prisoners, rescind security laws and halt rights violations (S)

2002-08-17.title: Pope Is in Poland, With Each Hoping to Do the Other Good
2002-08-17.abstract: Pope John Paul II arrives in Poland, where he acknowledges economic hardships Poles are enduring after nine years of brisk development and appears to add his voice to those criticizing government for not providing more assistance to people hurt by downturn; three-day visit will confine itself to Cracow area, underlining pope's much-diminished strength; stamp issued to commemorate visit calls it pope's seventh to Poland, even though he has visited nine times; 'mistake' makes stamps popular with collectors; map; photo (M)

2002-08-17.title: PRESIDENT NOTES DISSENT ON IRAQ, VOWING TO LISTEN
2002-08-17.abstract: Pres Bush says that he is listening carefully to group of Republicans who are warning him against going to war with Iraq, but that he will still make up his own mind based on information that is very tightly held within his administration; comment comes day after Brent Scowcroft, first Pres Bush's national security adviser and close Bush family friend, writes opinion article in Wall Street Journal warning that attack on Iraq at this time will jeopardize global counterterrorist campaign; one senior White House official says Scowcroft's views clearly represent views of president's father (M)

2002-08-18.title: Seeking Votes, German Leader Endorses Plan To Aid Jobless
2002-08-18.abstract: Chancellor Gerhard Schroder, trailing in German election polls, embraces proposals intended to halve unemployment, persistent economic ill that is dragging down his campaign; plan is already watered down by unions that back Schroder, while conservatives promise own plan to come (M)

2002-08-18.title: Indian Starts A Campaign Against Cash For Militants
2002-08-18.abstract: Shabnam Hashmi comes from New Delhi to US in international campaign imploring Indians abroad not to send money to militant Hindu organizations that she says are leading country away from secularism and into religious violence; visit was spurred by Hindu attacks on Muslims in Gujarat in February, in which hundreds of people died (M)

2002-08-18.title: Flooding Elbe Batters Towns Along Its Path
2002-08-18.abstract: Elbe river dams break, inundating German towns upriver from Dessau; photo of rescuers in Meissen; map (M)

2002-08-18.title: OFFICERS SAY U.S. AIDED IRAQ IN WAR DESPITE USE OF GAS
2002-08-18.abstract: Unidentified senior US military officers say covert US program during Reagan administration provided Iraq with critical battle planning assistance at time when US intelligence agencies knew Iraqi commanders would employ chemical weapons against Iran; comments respond to query about gas warfare on both sides during Iran-Iraq war; Iraq's use of gas is repeatedly cited by Bush administration in calling for regime change now; covert program took place as top Reagan administration officials, including George Shultz, Frank Carlucci and Colin Powell, publicly condemned Iraq for using poison gas, notably against Kurds; US at time sought to thwart Iran so it would not overrun oil-rich Persian Gulf states; Powell denies account; Saudi Arabia played key role in urging US to help Iraq; military informants say Lt Col Rick Francona toured Fao Peninsula after it was retaken by Iraq in early 1988 and reported that Iraq had used chemical weapons to win; map (M)

2002-08-18.title: Afghan Defense Chief Acts To Counter Talk of a Rift
2002-08-18.abstract: New Afghan government, under US pressure to heal damaging political rifts, has defense minister Muhammad Fahim make rare public appearance to deny power struggle with Pres Hamid Karzai; photo; Fahim is powerful Tajik leader seen as key rival to Karzai (M)

2002-08-19.title: Terror Leader Is Dead, Palestinian Reports Say
2002-08-19.abstract: Abu Nidal, a Palestinian linked to bombings and assassinations around the world, was found dead in Iraq, a Palestinian newspaper reported today.

2002-08-19.title: Bush Calls Security Meeting at Ranch
2002-08-19.abstract: Pres Bush will meet at ranch in Crawford, Tex, with top national security advisers, including Vice Pres Dick Cheney and Defense Sec Donald H Rumsfeld, and they are likely to discuss potential US military campaign to topple Iraq's Pres Saddam Hussein (S)

2002-08-19.title: Fighters' Demands Open Old Wounds In Central America
2002-08-19.abstract: Thousands of civil patrollers who committed atrocities at height of Guatemala's civil war in early 1980's are seeking payment from government for deadly service, alarming many in country where 36-year war ended with mostly unfulfilled promises for victims and nothing for patrollers except their demobilization; say they were forced under threat of death to leave farms and villages to work with army in routing leftist guerrillas; many see themselves as pawns cold war who were useful in holding Communism at bay then cast aside when wars ended as world's attention turned elsewhere; have not given up old networks, despite demobilization, raising fear that they could re-emerge to resolve complaints with weapons and anger amid festering social and economic problems; map; photo (M)

2002-08-19.title: Flood Damage May Force Czechs To Abandon Jet Fighter Purchase
2002-08-19.abstract: Czech Republic, new member of NATO, will likely abandon plan to spend $2 billion to buy jet fighters from British and Swedish consortium as country struggles to cope with costs of cleaning up from huge floods (S)

2002-08-19.title: Israel Will Start Pullout in Gaza And Bethlehem
2002-08-19.abstract: Israeli and Palestinian officials agree that Israeli troops will begin trial withdrawal from reoccupied territory in Gaza Strip and Bethlehem, in West Bank, on condition that Palestinians take responsibility for reducing tensions in areas; agreement, reached in four-hour meeting between Israeli defense minister, Benjamin Eliezer, and Palestinian Authority minister, Abdel Razak Yehiyeh, is first effort at cease-fire in current conflict negotiated directly between Israel and Palestinians; map (M)

2002-08-20.title: U.S. Explores A New World Of Warfare
2002-08-20.abstract: American forces complete largest joint war-fighting exercise ever held; three-week, $250 million operation involved 13,500 military and civilian personnel battling in nine live exercise ranges across US and twice as many computer simulations; results from exercise, planned for two years, are expected to shape planning against future adversaries; exercise described; photo (M)

2002-08-20.title: World Briefing | Africa: Zambia: Ex-President In Limbo
2002-08-20.abstract: Zambian high court judge postpones ruling on whether former president, Frederick Chiluba, can be stripped of immunity from prosecution; Chiluba has been accused of presiding over widespread corruption during 10 years in office; photo (S)

2002-08-20.title: World Briefing | Asia: Indonesia: Conciliatory Move Toward Separatists
2002-08-20.abstract: Indonesian government, in surprising move, says it wishes to continue negotiations with separatists in Aceh province rather than declare military emergency (S)

2002-08-20.title: GIANT HELICOPTER CRASHES IN RUSSIA
2002-08-20.abstract: Giant Mi-26 Russian military helicopter carrying at least 132 people crash-lands in minefield near main military base at Khankala in Chechnya, killing dozens; 32 survivors reportedly taken to military hospitals; other survivors apparently died after activating mines on emerging from wreckage; accident appears to be worst air disaster since Russia began second war in Chechnya three years ago; initial reports indicated helicopter was shot down by Chechen rebels, but military officials say pilot reported engine fire; rescue effort was delayed until explosives experts could clear path for emergency workers through mines, which encircle base; maps (M)

2002-08-20.title: German Ex-Communists Cut Adrift
2002-08-20.abstract: Gregor Gysi, charismatic head of Germany's Communist Party, abruptly resigns in scandal over his use of frequent-flyer miles earned on job; move leaves party without visible or widely recognized spokesman just weeks before national election and suddenly places its representation in federal Parliament at risk; photo (M)

2002-08-21.title: World Briefing | Americas: Mexico: People Smugglers Charged In Deaths
2002-08-21.abstract: Federal prosecutors file organized-crime charges against 11 accused smugglers who abandoned 25 Mexicans in Arizona desert in May 2001; indictments follow raid and arrests in Mexican border town of Nogales; 14 of Mexicans died (S)

2002-08-21.title: Bush Team Is Campaigning to Muster Opposition in Africa to Mugabe's Rule
2002-08-21.abstract: Bush administration says it is working to foster opposition to Pres Robert Mugabe in Zimbabwe, calling his rule illegitimate and irrational; says he won re-election in March through fraud and coercion and has since helped spread starvation in southern Africa through misguided land redistribution plan under which hundreds of skilled white farmers are being evicted from their property; says farms are not being given to landless blacks but to loyal military officers and Mugabe's cronies (M)

2002-08-21.title: Soham Journal; Girls' Killing Melts British Reserve
2002-08-21.abstract: Britain is deeply stirred by murders of Holly Wells and Jessica Chapman, two 10-year-old girls abducted in Soham, town of 8,000 that is 70 miles north of London; Ian Huntley, 28-year-old high school custodian, is charged with murder in both cases; Maxine Carr, 25-year-old classroom assistant at victims' own school, is charged with conspiring to pervert cause of justice; suspects were known as last people to have seen girls alive on Aug 4, and they were giving television interviews a day before their arrest on Aug 17; photos; map (M)

2002-08-21.title: World Briefing: Europe
2002-08-21.abstract: SWITZERLAND: Ill-Gotten Gains Returned To Peru; THE NETHERLANDS: Exclusive Guest List For Milosevic; IRELAND: Tax Reduces Use Of Plastic Bags;.

2002-08-21.title: CNN Now Says It Paid $30,000 For Its Videotapes of bin Laden
2002-08-21.abstract: CNN says it paid about $30,000 to people in Afghanistan for videotapes of Osama bin Laden and operatives of Al Qaeda it is now televising; previously indicated that it did not pay for them; insists that money did not go to anyone connected with bin Laden or Qaeda (M)

2002-08-22.title: State Dept. Backing Pakistan Leader Despite New Powers
2002-08-22.abstract: The State Dept. said today that the U.S. believed that President Pervez Musharraf was committed to democracy in Pakistan

2002-08-22.title: A 'PATIENT' BUSH SAYS HE'LL WEIGH ALL IRAQ OPTIONS
2002-08-22.abstract: Pres Bush calls himself 'patient man' who would take his time and consider all options, including nonmilitary ways, before determining how to proceed to replace Saddam Hussein as leader of Iraq; denies that war planning was discussed during two-hour midvacation meeting held at his ranch with Vice Pres Cheney, Defense Sec Rumsfeld and Gen Richard B Myers, chairman of joint Chiefs of Staff; hours later, Rumsfeld tells large gathering of Army troops at nearby Fort Hood, Tex, that such war planning is clearly on Bush's mind; Rep Tom DeLay, House majority whip, describes Hussein as growing threat to American security and declares that US must attack him 'the sooner, the better'; White House aides express concern about growing speculations over what Bush might do about Iraq and when, and about suggestions of divisions in Republican Party over Bush's plans for Baghdad (M)

2002-08-22.title: Kapsait Journal; A Tourist Spot With No Stars, but Many Medals
2002-08-22.abstract: Many of Kenya's, and world's, top long-distance runners train in Kapsait and surrounding Rift Valley countryside in western highlands; Kenya tourism officials are hoping to turn their running camps into tourist attractions; their idea is to exploit thrill of tagging along with runners as they train; runners say they would enjoy having tourists around, if only to relieve feeling of isolation that envelops them in between runs; much work must be done before tourists can be attracted to camps, some of which lack running water or electricity and many of which are in remote villages at end of long rugged roads; phtos; map (M)

2002-08-22.title: World Briefing | Europe: Russia: Member Of Parliament Slain
2002-08-22.abstract: Vladimir I Golovlev, member of Russia's lower house of Parliament, is slain, Moscow, latest victim in series of killings of city, state and federal officials; is first member of Parliament killed since 1998; Sergei N Yushenkov, leader of Golovlev's party, calls killing politically motivated (S)

2002-08-22.title: 5 Who Attacked Iraqi Embassy Had Sought Asylum in Germany
2002-08-22.abstract: Five men who seized Iraqi Embassy in Berlin appear in court there and are described as recent asylum seekers in Germany; top Iraqi diplomat in Germany, Shamil al-Muhammad, calls them 'paid mercenaries' and claims Israel and United States were behind their actions; five hostage-takers, calling themselves Democratic Iraqi Opposition of Germany, called for end of 'terrorist regime of Saddam Hussein'; Iraqi opposition groups, including main Iraqi National Congress headed by Ahmad Chalabi, say they do not know men or group they say they represent; men's names have not been released (M)

2002-08-23.title: Jakarta Journal; A TV Preacher to Satisfy the Taste for Islam Lite
2002-08-23.abstract: Abdullah Gymnastiar, Indonesia's most popular Islamic preacher, has television show during which he answers viewers' questions on practical matters; his emegence seen as proof that Indonesians wear their religion lightly and are profoundly moderate in their practice of Islam; Gymnastiar keeps politics out of his sermons, with exception of constantly upbraiding Indonesian leaders for being corrupt; his reluctance to accept invitation to travel to United States seen as reflecting suspicions about US in Indonesia; photo; map (M)

2002-08-23.title: Foreign Prisoners Becoming a Problem for Karzai
2002-08-23.abstract: As many as 4,000 prisoners captured by American-backed Northern Alliance forces remain in prisons around Afghanistan, presenting growing problem for Pres Hamid Karzai; Pakistan, home of largest group of prisoners, is demanding that Afghanistan begin repatriating them; Karzai has pledged to do so after prisoners are vetted to identify hard-core Taliban or Qaeda members; several hundred Pakistanis have been released since spring, but International Committee of Red Cross says as many as 1,200 Pakistani prisoners remain; their plight is political issue in Pakistan, where Islamic clericsresponded to Sept 11 by whipping up militant fervor and sending thousands of men and teenagers to militant training camps along border, from where they trekked into Afghanistan; 13 prisoners from Pakistan who tried to escape on Aug 7 were shot dead by Afghan forces, causing furor in Pakistan; photos; map (M)

2002-08-23.title: Putin Dresses Down Military For Crash That Killed 116
2002-08-23.abstract: Pres Vladimir V Putin sharply criticizes his military commanders, bluntly blaming dereliction of duty for catastrophic crash of overloaded transport helicopter near main military base in Chechnya; notes that Mi-26 helicopter was being used to shuttle troops in and out of Chechnya even though Ministry of Defense had banned its use for anything but cargo in 1997; number of deaths in crash rises to 116 as another soldier dies of injuries; Putin's stinging remarks seen as clearest indication yet of his growing frustration with internal resistance to his proposals to make military leaner, more agile and more professional; independent military analyst says order barring passengers on Mi-26 was disregarded in Chechnya because soldiers feared traveling overland (M)

2002-08-23.title: Nuclear Material Secretly Flown From Serbia to Russia for Safety
2002-08-23.abstract: United States officials say more than 100 pounds of nuclear material considered at risk of being stolen or sold for use in producing nuclear weapons was secretly flown from Serbia to Russia, where it will be processed for use in commercial power plant; operation was financed by US and private foundation, involving 1,200 Serbian soldiers and scores of international observers; more than 5,000 rods of highly enriched uranium was transported from research reactor near Belgrade to Russia; American officials and nculear experts are hailing highly classified project as model of international cooperation and public-private financing that could be repeated in other Eastern and Central European countries that have bomb-grade nuclear materials considered at risk of being stolen or sold (M)

2002-08-23.title: North Korea's Leader Whistle-Stops in Siberia
2002-08-23.abstract: Kim Jong Il of North Korea will meet with Pres Vladimir V Putin of Russia to discuss Russian plan to extend Trans-Siberian Railroad through Korea; rail route through North Korea to South Korea would cut costs and shipping times in half for containers that now go by sea from South Korea to Europe; projected half million South Korean containers a year could push Trans-Siberian Railroad into black; one stumbling block could be North Korea's military, which would be loathe to lose strategic advantage of bottling up South Korea on southern end of peninsula; photo; map (M)

2002-08-24.title: Expat Adviser : Cutting the cost of getting mail from home
2002-08-24.abstract: I am living in Paris but I need a U.S. address to receive and then forward my letters and magazines to me. Do you know of a reliable and reasonably priced service? — Susan Ehrlich, Paris

2002-08-24.title: Politics in Moscow More Dagger Than Cloak
2002-08-24.abstract: Russia's elected officials and political appointees are killed with alarming regularity, not because of their politics usually, but rather because they either thwarted organized crime or were in league with it; that crime figures do hesitate to kill politicians and that remarkably few political assassins are ever caught are testaments to frailty of Russia's justice system and depth of official corruption; photo (M)

2002-08-24.title: Chinese Hold Their Breath as a Swollen Lake Recedes
2002-08-24.abstract: Water levels in Dongting Lake in central China begin to recede, encouraging hope that any major disaster from summer's most menacing flood may be averted; potentially catastrophic seepages in mainly earthen dikes and embankments have been spotted by roving teams of farmers and sealed by troops with sandbags and rocks; flood control officials downstream from lake, along Yangtze River in Hubei and Anhui Provinces, brace for oncoming rush of water; photo; map (M)

2002-08-24.title: Money matters : In Australia, expats face a taxing time
2002-08-24.abstract: When new residents in Australia begin paying taxes, they probably will find that their adopted country is not just distant but expensive.

2002-08-24.title: At 100, Hitler's Filmmaker Sticks to Her Script
2002-08-24.abstract: Interview with Leni Riefenstahl, now 100 years old, who continues to plead naivete or to invoke a failing memory when questioned about propaganda films she made for Hitler; photo (M)

2002-08-25.title: State Department Will Take Over Security for Afghan Leader
2002-08-25.abstract: State Dept security officers will begin providing inner ring of bodyguards for Pres Hamid Karzai in Sept, as they take over that job from United States Special Oeprations forces; American military will continue to assign forces to form outer ring of security for Karzai, and will supply intelligence and logistical aid to State Dept detail (M)

2002-08-25.title: U.S. Coordinates Drug Sweep in the Balkans and Central Asia
2002-08-25.abstract: Thousands of suspects are detained or arrested in broad narcotics sweep involving25,000 law enforcement officers and coordinated by Drug Enforcement Administration across 15 countries of Central Asia and Balkans (M)

2002-08-25.title: Dike Patrols Expand as Lake in China Springs More Leaks
2002-08-25.abstract: Citizen patrols step up along hundreds of miles of dikes that hem in huge, swollen Dongting Lake in central China; officials say weather upstream during coming week would determine whether flooding threat and need for vigilance along dikes should abate or be prolonged (M)

2002-08-25.title: Saudi Sought By the F.B.I. Is Said to Be In Custody
2002-08-25.abstract: Saud A S al-Rasheed, 21-year-old Saudi man sought by FBI for possible links to Sept 11 hijackers, has turned himself in to authorities in his homeland and denied any involvement with terrorist conspiracy; man's father, Abdulaziz al-Rasheed, says his son has no ties to Al Qaeda and cannot understand why his photo turned up among others of Sept 11 hijackers; American law officials say FBI issued worldwide alert for Rasheed after agents reviewed compact disc seized by Pakistani authorities that contained image of his passport and photos of some of 19 hijackers (M)

2002-08-25.title: AIDS Scourge in Rural China Leaves Villages of Orphans
2002-08-25.abstract: AIDS is creating explosion of destitute orphans in China's rural heartland and is driving large numbers of families into such dire poverty that they can no longer afford to feed or clothes their children; in small village of Donghu there are 20 orphans where last year there were none, all because of AIDS; residents estimate that 200 of village's 600 families have one parent dead and other ill, often too frail to work or even rise from bed; they receive little government help; experts say blow dealt by AIDS to villages like Donghu has been sharper and crueler than anywhere else in world because of unusual and efficient way disease is spread there; in 1990's, nearly entire adult population of some villages was infected almost simultaneously as poor farmers flocked en masse to sell their blood at blood collection stations whose unsterile practices introduced hefty doses of HIV directly into their veins; now victims are falling ill and dying, almost in unison; selling blood was not banned in China until mid-90's; photos; map (L)

2002-08-26.title: Pope Decides Against Trip to Philippines
2002-08-26.abstract: A Vatican official said today that the primary reason for Pope John Paul II deciding not to go to the Philippines in January was his health.

2002-08-26.title: Indonesia struggles to curb rapid deforestation
2002-08-26.abstract: JAKARTA—  Almost every scientific expedition that sets out to explore Indonesia's forests returns with discoveries of new species. The world's fourth most populous nation has the third largest tropical forest, after Brazil and the Democratic Republic of the Congo.

2002-08-26.title: Central Asian University Aims to Train Region's Next Leaders
2002-08-26.abstract: Group of international scholars is creating University of Central Asia, regional university to bring secular education to people in some of remotest, most forbidding and poverty-stricken regions; university will offer degree programs based on liberal arts and sciences curriculum, unlike specialist programs of Soviet era; will initially have campuses in Tajikistan, Kyrgyzstan and Kazakhstan; has been established by treaty ratified by Parliaments of three countries; programs are being created to serve new elite in hope they will provide leadership in region; maps (M)

2002-08-26.title: IRAQ SAID TO PLAN TANGLING THE U.S. IN STREET FIGHTING
2002-08-26.abstract: Pentagon officials and former US government experts say Pres Saddam Hussein of Iraq will try to compensate for his armed forces' glaring weaknesses by raising specter of urban warfare if Bush administration moves to launch military strike; say Iraq has already started military preparations in anticipation of military strike; forces have been digging defensive positions for military equipment around Baghdad; have been moving air defense units around country and dispersing army units in field to make them less vulnerable to surprise attack; current and fomer US military officers express confidence US will ultimately triumph, but differ about how difficult military campaign involving urban warfare would be; photos; map (M)

2002-08-26.title: France's nuclear dilemma:power vs. environment
2002-08-26.abstract: PARIS—  France faces some tough decisions on nuclear power that crystallize the dilemma of how to balance prosperity with the environment, the core issue at the World Summit on Sustainable Development in Johannesburg.

2002-08-27.title: World Development Forum Begins With a Rebuke
2002-08-27.abstract: Tens of thousands of officials, environmentalists and advocates for poor converge on Johannesburg, South Africa, for UN World Summit on Sustainable Development to devise an ambitious blueprint to promote development while protecting natural resources; participants express doubts about sincerity of developed countries; are especially critical of fact that Pres Bush, leader of world's biggest economy and its largest polluter, decided not to attend; are addressed by South African Pres Thabo Mbeki, who says world has stood by and allowed environmental degradation and deepening poverty to threaten future of Earth; he accuses world leaders of failing to muster political will to reduce poverty and to protect natural resources despite earlier promises; photo (M)

2002-08-27.title: In Talk With Envoy, Bush Assails Hussein
2002-08-27.abstract: President Bush told Saudi Arabia's ambassador to the U.S. that Saddam Hussein was "a menace and a threat" to both his Middle East neighbors and the U.S.

2002-08-27.title: Past Haunts a Forum On Global Ecology
2002-08-27.abstract: Tens of thousands of officials, environmentalists and advocates for the poor converged in Johannesburg for the United Nations' World Summit on Sustainable Development to devise an ambitious blueprint to promote development while protecting natural resources.     They vowed to hammer out a plan to clean polluted air and to help people escape from poverty.

2002-08-27.title: World Briefing | Asia: China: North Koreans' Protest Foiled
2002-08-27.abstract: Chinese police quickly detain small group of North Korean refugees trying to hold protest outside foreign ministry (S)0

2002-08-27.title: American Gives Beijing Good News: Rebels on Terror List
2002-08-27.abstract: Bush administration lists East Turkmenistan Islamic Movement, obscure Muslim group fighting Chinese rule in western province of Xinjiang, as terrorist organization; decision, announced by visiting Deputy Sec of State Richard L Armitage, pleases Beijing, which is anxious to portray crackdown on Uighur Muslims in Xinjiang as part of global campaign against terrorism; may bolster China's cooperation in American-led campaign; photo (M)

2002-08-28.title: World Briefing | Asia: Turkmenistan: Military Aid From U.S.
2002-08-28.abstract: Gen Tommy R Franks says US plans to expand military cooperation with Turkmenistan; photo (S)

2002-08-28.title: Saving Water, U.S. Farmers Are Worried They'll Parch
2002-08-28.abstract: Fourth article in Running Dry series on competition for water around the world focuses on farmers who depend on Ogallala aquifer, which spans eight American states, from South Dakota to Texas, and has declined sharply in some areas over last 50 years; farmers began saving water through technology and conservation even before recent years of drought, and they, together with industrial users, are largely responsible for significant decline in America's per-capital water use; farmers have primary water rights in most of country, but competition from fast-growing cities and suburbs is intensifying; Ronnie Hopper, who grows cotton in Petersburg, Tex, argues like many farmers that cities have no claim on aquifers at all; photos; map; graph (L)

2002-08-28.title: World Briefing | Europe: Russia: Member Of Parliament Attacked
2002-08-28.abstract: Two assailants seriously injure Magomet Tekeyev, member of Russia's Parliament, and kill his aide in attack outside legislator's home in southwestern republic of Karachayev-Cherkessia (S)

2002-08-28.title: In Talk With Envoy, Bush Assails Hussein, but Saudis Are Firm in Opposing War
2002-08-28.abstract: Pres Bush entertains Prince Bandar bin Sultan, Saudi Arabia's US ambassador, at Texas ranch, where he tells him that Saddam Hussein of Iraq is menance and threat to both his neighbors and US, but Saudis stick to their position that war is not acceptable and that they will not cooperate in any military action; several administration officials are taken by surprise by vehemence of Vice Pres Dick Cheney's Aug 26 speech on necessity for military action against Iraq, and it is unclear whether his comments mean that administration is no longer interested in getting weapons inspectors back into Iraq; Pres Hosni Mubarak of Egypt says that if US strikes at Iraqi people because of one or two individuals and leaves Palestinian issue unresolved, not a single ruler in the Arab world will be able to curb popular sentiment; Bush also urges Saudi Arabia to free Amjad Radwan, 19-year-old American woman who was taken to Saudi territory by her father as child and is not permitted to leave without her father's written permission; Saudi courts almost always rule in favor of Saudi fathers in child custody cases (M)

2002-08-28.title: For North Korean, It Seems Possible to Have a Hot Time in Siberia
2002-08-28.abstract: North Korean government and news agency take pains to describe their leader Kim Jong Il's current rail trip across Siberia as hard work even as new book by Konstantin Pulikovsky portrays similar trip Kim took in 2001 as sybaritic excursion (M)

2002-08-29.title: Iran's President Trying to Limit Power of Clergy
2002-08-29.abstract: Pres Mohammad Khatami of Iran says hard-line clerics make it all but impossible for him to do his job; says he will propose legislation to restore powers that Iran's president has lost to clergy; his statement seen as clear expression of frustration with clerics who hold most real levers of power and have thwarted president elected twice on promises to open economy and usher in greater civil liberties; says his dialogue with hard-liners has proved fruitless; his challenge suggests new willingness to confront them more forcefully; it seems certain to intensify long power struggle in Iran; his proposals seem certain to pass in Parliament, where his supporters hold majority; but any changes can be blocked by religious panel called Guardian Council, which has turned back reforms before; Khatami says he has already presented Parliament with bill to limit powers of council, and will soon send bill to restore presidential powers that would allow him to act as head of state, enforce Constitution and protect citizens' rights; says public disaffection is rising, stemming mostly from his government's failure to bring citizens greater freedom; photo (M)

2002-08-29.title: Licensing of Gas Stations Ignites Patronage Scandal in India
2002-08-29.abstract: Prime Min Atal Bihari Vajpayee has canceled more than 3,000 allotments for gas stations and cooking gas outlets awarded by Indian government since Jan 2000, in effort to contain patronage scandal; newspaper investigation has found that bulk of licenses have gone to members of governing party and their friends and relatives and party's Hindu nationalist allies (M)

2002-08-29.title: Worried Saudis Pay Millions To Improve Image in the U.S.
2002-08-29.abstract: Saudi government has spent millions of dollars on lobbyists and national television advertisements since Sept 11 in drive to improve its image among Americans and is poised to spend more as anniversary of events approaches; Saudi officials say they want to counter intensified anger or skepticism among Americans toward their country, which was home to 15 of 19 hijackers and which has clashed publicly with Bush administration over possible attack against Iraq; royal family is scrambling to find gesture of solidarity with American people on anniversary of attacks, in striking sign of Saudis' eagerness to reach out to US; toward that end, Saudis have hired several public relations firms and already spent more than $5 million, according to new Justice Dept filings; Adel al-Jubeir, adviser to Saudi government and key figure in new effort, says his government is appropriating techniques of American political campaign to overhaul its image in US (M)

2002-08-29.title: Buoyed by World's Focus on Terror, Spain Cracks Down in Basque Region
2002-08-29.abstract: Government of Prime Min Jose Maria Aznar of Spain has seized on new international support for fight against terrorism to toughen its already hard line against Basque violence; it has stepped up political and police pressure on separatist group ETA; Spanish Judge Baltasar Garzon is pressing his own long-standing campaign against separatist violence, moving to seize $23 million in assets of radical party Batasuna; he has argued that Batasuna acts on ETA's orders or to finance its attacks; government's new actions go well beyond its previous antiterrorism stance, marking end to policy of accommodation with more moderate Basque nationalists that Spanish leaders have generally followed since 1975; photo; graph from Spain's interior minister showing numbers of killings from ETA violence each year, totaling 812 since 1968 (M)

2002-08-29.title: Germans Issue First Indictment in the 9/11 Plot
2002-08-29.abstract: German prosecutors bring charges against Mounir el-Motassadeq, Moroccan man accused of supporting members of Hamburg cell suspected of helping to plan and carry out Sept 11 attacks on United States; this is first formal criminal charge brought by German authorities in connection with attacks; Motassadeq was arrested in Nov on evidence that he had managed bank account of one of suicide hijackers and arranged wire transfers to hijackers while they were learning to fly in US; was one of witnesses who in 1996 signed will of Mohamed Atta, suspected ringleader of plot; has always admitted knowing hijackers, but he has provided reporters with various versions of nature and intensity of his contacts with them (M)

2002-08-30.title: Administration Seeking to Build Support in Congress on Iraq Issue
2002-08-30.abstract: Bush administration seeks to shore up Congressional support for campaign against Iraq, even as it tries to head off pressure from Britain and France to work through United Nations before taking any action against Saddam Hussein; Vice Pres Dick Cheney says administration would 'consult widely with our Congress' before deciding whether to go to war; does not make clear whether administration would seek formal vote on authorization of war; seeks to undercut arguments of critics who urge return to United Nations weapons inspections, saying inspectors in past failed to uncover evidence that Iraq was in fact continuing to mass-produce deadly chemical agents; Security Council diplomats say United States has backed away from engaging in Council's pressure on Iraq about inspectors, with American diplomats calling such efforts increasingly fruitless; no other Security Council nations appear ready to give up on inspections process; photos (M)

2002-08-30.title: World Briefing | Asia: Cambodia: Floods Leave 14 Dead
2002-08-30.abstract: Floodwaters begin receding in Cambodia's north, but are rising in south; 14 people are dead (S)

2002-08-30.title: World Briefing | Africa: Nigeria: Death Sentence For Extramarital Sex
2002-08-30.abstract: Islamic court in Nigerian state sentences man and woman to death for engaging in extramarital sex (S)

2002-08-30.title: Kenyan Leader Fires His Deputy in Revolt
2002-08-30.abstract: President Daniel arap Moi fired his vice president today in an attempt to quash a revolt within his ruling party.

2002-08-30.title: Destitution Seen for Palestinians
2002-08-30.abstract: The senior U.N. official in the region on Thursday painted a devastating picture of the Palestinian economy in which a large majority was "scrambling to survive" on less than $2 a day.

2002-08-31.title: World Briefing | Middle East: Iran: No Fun Allowed
2002-08-31.abstract: Police arrest 17 young men and women for holding party, listening to illegal music and attempted bribery, near Bandar Anzali, Iran; mingling of sexes and partying are crimes in Iran (S)

2002-08-31.title: World Briefing | Asia: Pakistan: Bhutto Thwarted In Election Bid
2002-08-31.abstract: Local election official in southern Pakistan rejects bid by ex-Prime Min Benazir Bhutto to register for parliamentary elections in October; her supporters vow appeal; photo (S)

2002-08-31.title: World Briefing | Asia: China: Life Sentences For Internet Cafe Fire
2002-08-31.abstract: Two teen-age boys convicted of arson in June fire that killed 25 in crowded Internet cafe, Beijing, are sentenced to life in prison; girl who helped them gets 12-year sentence (S)

2002-08-31.title: Dispute Over New Court Perils Widened Alliance
2002-08-31.abstract: Struggle between Bush administration and European over new International Criminal Court is placing some candidate nations for both European Union and NATO in severe political bind; United States presses EU to let individual nations sign bilateral accords with US to exempt Americans from court's jurisdiction; some EU foreign ministers, meeting, Denmark, are insisting that union reject such exemptions and continue to urge 10 candidate nations to do same; candidate nations like Estonia, Latvia and Lithuania would like to wait to see if EU and US can work out compromise, which Britain is now trying to broker; American legislators have told Baltic leaders that Senate approval of their NATO membership depends on their signing waivers; State Department has denied waiver is condition for NATO membership (M)

2002-08-31.title: Kenya's Retiring Leader Fires Vice President Over Succession
2002-08-31.abstract: Kenya Pres Daniel arap Moi, 77, fires Vice Pres George Saitoti in effort to quash revolt within his ruling party among top aides opposed to his choice of Uhuru Kenyatta as his successor; Saitoti is among many presidential aspirants in Moi's party in December election; Kenyatta has relatively little political experience, but is son of founding president, Jomo Kenyatta (M)
